content.assessment: From a whole-system reform perspective, capacity contraction without changing the tracking age or selection criteria addresses provision volume but not the comprehensive-schooling principle itself. Finland's reform succeeded by replacing the dual-track structure entirely, not by shrinking one track's capacity while leaving its selective logic and age threshold intact. The Finnish experience with informal sorting ('emphasized classes') and school-choice segregation also suggests that a smaller selective sector does not eliminate the underlying stratifying mechanism, since demand and advantage can concentrate more intensely in fewer places, or migrate to less-regulated maintainer types.
opportunities: Could reduce the absolute scale of early-selection exposure even without structural change, a partial mitigation in the spirit of reducing between-school variance (F-live-finnish_reform-06)
strengths: Uses precedent of existing discretionary ministerial power, a plausible administratively durable lever comparable in kind (if not scope) to state-level administrative control mechanisms
threats: Displaced demand into non-state, less-regulated maintainers mirrors the pattern by which Finnish school choice generated segregation through middle-class sorting behavior operating around formal constraints, suggesting contraction alone risks merely relocating rather than reducing stratification (F-live-finnish_reform-09)
weaknesses: Does not touch tracking age or comprehensive provision, the two dimensions the Finnish case identifies as central to the equity outcome (F-live-finnish_reform-06)

Contraction only within state-sector jurisdiction leaves a large exempted space (church/private) analogous to how Finnish school-choice effects operate partly outside formal comprehensive-system control (F-live-finnish_reform-09)
